document_text:
Unlocking High-Fidelity Analog Joint
Source-Channel Coding on Standard Digital
Transceivers
Shumin Yao1, Hao Chen1, Yaping Sun1, Nan Ma2,1,Member, IEEE,Xiaodong Xu2,1,Senior Member, IEEE,
Qinglin Zhao3,Senior Member, IEEE,Shuguang Cui4,Fellow IEEE
1Department of Broadband Communication, Pengcheng Laboratory, Shenzhen 518066, China
2State Key Laboratory of Networking and Switching Technology, Beijing University of Posts and
Telecommunications, Beijing 100876, China
3School of Computer Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology,
Avenida Wei Long, Taipa, Macau, China
4Future Nework of Intelligent Institute (FNii),
The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen), Shenzhen, China
{yaoshm, chenh03, sunyp}@pcl.ac.cn,{manan, xuxiaodong}@bupt.edu.cn,
qlzhao@must.edu.mo, shuguangcui@cuhk.edu.cn
Abstract—Analog joint source-channel coding (JSCC) has
demonstrated superior performance for semantic communica-
tions through graceful degradation across channel conditions.
However, a fundamental hardware-software mismatch prevents
deployment on modern digital physical layers (PHYs): analog
JSCC generates continuous-valued symbols requiring infinite
waveform diversity, while digital PHYs produce a finite set of
discrete waveforms and employ non-differentiable operations that
break end-to-end gradient flow. Existing solutions either funda-
mentally limit representation granularity or require impractical
white-box PHY access. We introduce D2AJSCC, a novel frame-
work enabling high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard
digital PHYs. Our approach exploits orthogonal frequency-
division multiplexing’s parallel subcarrier structure as a wave-
form synthesizer: computational PHY inversion determines input
bitstreams that orchestrate subcarrier amplitudes and phases to
emulate ideal analog waveforms. To enable end-to-end training
despite non-differentiable PHY operations, we develop Prox-
yNet—a differentiable neural surrogate of the communication
link that provides uninterrupted gradient flow while preventing
JSCC degeneration. Simulation results for image transmission
over WiFi PHY demonstrate that our system achieves near-ideal
analog JSCC performance with graceful degradation across SNR
conditions, while baselines exhibit cliff effects or catastrophic
failures. By enabling next-generation semantic transmission on
legacy infrastructure without hardware modification, our frame-
work promotes sustainable network evolution and bridges the
critical gap between analog JSCC’s theoretical promise and
practical deployment on ubiquitous digital hardware.
Index Terms—Joint source-channel coding, Semantic commu-
nication, Wireless communication
I. INTRODUCTION
Driven by rapid advancements in artificial intelligence,
semantic communication has emerged as a transformative
paradigm in wireless communications [1]. A key enabling
technology is deep-learning-based joint source-channel coding
(JSCC) [2], where a deep neural network (DNN) encoderextracts essential semantic information from source messages
while a corresponding decoder reconstructs them. By jointly
optimizing this end-to-end process, JSCC robustly delivers
information over noisy channels with minimal overhead,
surpassing traditional separate source and channel coding
schemes.
Among various JSCC approaches, analog JSCC has proven
particularly effective [3]. It compresses high-dimensional
sources into compact, continuous-valued symbols that capture
fine-grained semantic nuances and are optimized for channel
robustness. Pioneering work demonstrated significant perfor-
mance gains for image transmission [4], spurring extensions
to various modalities and scenarios. The hallmark of analog
JSCC isgraceful degradation: performance smoothly scales
with channel quality rather than exhibiting the catastrophic
“cliff effect” of digital systems.
However, a critical gap exists between analog JSCC’s
theoretical promise and practical deployment. These models
assume anidealized analog-native physical layer (PHY)that
directly transmits continuous-valued waveforms. Modern hard-
ware, however, is built ondigital-centric PHYsengineered
to process discrete bit sequences. This hardware-software
mismatch creates two fundamental deployment barriers.
Challenge 1: Signal format incompatibility.Continu-
ous JSCC symbols conceptually require infinite waveform
diversity, but digital PHYs accept only bits and pro-
duce a finite set of discrete waveforms. The conventional
workaround—quantizing symbols into bits—creates an in-
tractable trade-off: high-precision quantization preserves fi-
delity but generates large payloads that negate JSCC’s com-
pression benefits, while low-precision quantization maintains
efficiency but introduces catastrophic errors.
Challenge 2: Non-differentiable training barrier.JSCC’sarXiv:2603.09080v1  [cs.IT]  10 Mar 2026

power derives from end-to-end gradient-based optimization.
Digital PHYs contain non-differentiable operations (channel
coding, discrete modulation) that break gradient flow. Even
if addressable via gradient approximations [5], the PHY’s
internal channel coding forces the JSCC model to degenerate
into a simple source coder, recreating separate source-channel
coding and reintroducing the very cliff effect JSCC aims to
eliminate.
Existing solutions remain inadequate. Quantization-based
approaches [5], [6] still bottleneck performance through the
fundamental information loss of discretization. Digital JSCC
schemes attempt native compatibility but face their own
limitations. Modulation-aware methods [7], [8] require im-
practical “white-box” PHY access to disable channel coding
and directly control modulators—infeasible with integrated,
black-box commercial hardware. Feature quantization ap-
proaches [9], [10] convert semantics to discrete codebooks,
fundamentally limiting representation granularity.
This paper bridges the gap between analog JSCC’s con-
ceptual power and digital hardware’s practical constraints. We
introduce D2AJSCC, a novel framework enabling high-fidelity
analog JSCC deployment on standard digital PHYs for the
first time. This approach not only unlocks the robustness of
semantic communications but also enhances sustainability by
repurposing existing digital infrastructure for analog-native
protocols, thereby negating the need for costly hardware
replacements. We demonstrate the practical viability of this
framework by deploying an analog JSCC model for image
transmission over a standard WiFi PHY . Our key contributions
are:
•To address the signal format incompatibility (Challenge
1), we introduce a waveform emulation approach that
exploits the parallel subcarrier structure of orthogonal
frequency-division multiplexing based (OFDM-based)
digital PHYs as a flexible waveform synthesizer. Through
computational PHY inversion, we determine the precise
input bitstream that orchestrates subcarrier amplitudes
and phases to collectively emulate the ideal analog JSCC
waveform. This resolves the fidelity-efficiency impasse.
•To overcome the non-differentiable training barrier (Chal-
lenge 2), we develop a proxy-based training strategy.
We first prevent JSCC degeneration by using software-
defined modules (SDMs) to computationally invert the
PHY’s processing pipeline, rendering its channel coder
transparent and compelling true joint optimization. We
then introduce ProxyNet, a differentiable neural network
trained as a high-fidelity surrogate of the entire SDM-
enhanced communication link. During JSCC training, this
frozen proxy creates an uninterrupted gradient path across
non-differentiable hardware.
•We validate our framework via simulations of image
transmission over standard 802.11a WiFi PHY . Results
show our system nearly matches ideal analog JSCC
performance with graceful degradation across SNR con-
ditions, while quantization baselines exhibit cliff effects
or catastrophic failures.
EncoderDecoder
Channel
𝑠 𝑡  Fig. 1. A typical analog joint source-channel coding system for image
transmission.
The remainder of this paper presents our system model
and problem formulation (Sec. II), the proposed D2AJSCC
framework (Sec. III), experimental validation (Sec. IV), and
conclusions (Sec. V).
II. SYSTEMMODEL ANDPROBLEMFORMULATION
This section provides the necessary background on analog
JSCC and the WiFi PHY , then formulates the core deployment
challenges our framework addresses.
A. Analog Joint Source-Channel Coding
Analog JSCC jointly optimizes source and channel coding
through deep neural networks, enabling graceful degradation
where reconstruction quality degrades smoothly with channel
conditions rather than exhibiting catastrophic cliff effects.
As illustrated in Fig. 1, a DNN encoderf enccompresses
a source imageI∈RH×W×Cinto a compact real-valued
representationz∈R2K×1:
z=f enc(I;θ enc).(1)
These features are mapped toKcomplex symbolsS∈CK×1
by pairing consecutive elements:
Sk=z 2k−1 +j·z 2k, k= 1, . . . , K.(2)
The symbols are pulse-shaped into a continuous baseband
waveform for transmission:
s(t) =KX
k=1Sk·p(t−kT s),(3)
wherep(t)is a pulse-shaping filter andT sis the symbol
period. This direct symbol-to-waveform mapping is crucial
and assumes an analog-native PHY capable of transmitting
continuous-valued signals.
At the receiver, the distorted symbols ˆSare decomposed
back into real values ˆz∈R2K×1and fed to the decoder for
reconstruction:
ˆI=f dec(ˆz;θ dec).(4)
The system is trained end-to-end by minimizing reconstruction
error, typically mean squared error:

B. WiFi Physical Layer
The WiFi PHY operates on discrete bitstreams through a
fixed, non-differentiable processing chain, as shown in Fig. 2.
The transmitter transforms input bitsbthrough sequential
modules: scrambling (T 1), convolutional coding (T 2), inter-
leaving (T 3), quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM,T 4),
pilot insertion (T 5), and IFFT (T 6) to generate an OFDM
waveform. The receiver inverts this process through FFT (R 1),
channel equalization (R 2), QAM demodulation (R 3), deinter-
leaving (R 4), convolutional decoding (R 5), and descrambling
(R6) to recover ˆb.
C. Problem Formulation
Deploying analog JSCC over a standard WiFi PHY presents
two fundamental challenges stemming from the mismatch
between software-defined analog systems and hardware-based
digital architectures:
Challenge 1: Signal format incompatibility.Analog JSCC
generates continuous-valued symbols requiring infinite wave-
form diversity, while the WiFi PHY accepts only discrete bits
and produces a finite set of waveforms. Quantizing JSCC sym-
bols into bits creates an intractable trade-off: high-precision
quantization preserves fidelity but generates large payloads
that destroy transmission efficiency, while low-precision quan-
tization maintains efficiency but introduces catastrophic quan-
tization errors before transmission even begins.
Challenge 2: Non-differentiable training barrier.JSCC’s
performance depends on end-to-end gradient-based optimiza-
tion. The WiFi PHY contains non-differentiable operations
that break gradient flow, preventing training. More critically,
even if gradients could be approximated, the PHY’s internal
channel coding (T 2) forces JSCC to degenerate into a simple
source coder, recreating separate source-channel coding and
reintroducing the cliff effect that JSCC aims to eliminate.
Our objective is to design a framework that enables high-
fidelity analog JSCC deployment over standard WiFi PHY by
resolving both challenges while preserving graceful degrada-
tion and transmission efficiency.

III. PROPOSEDFRAMEWORK
We introduce a two-component framework that enables
analog JSCC deployment over standard WiFi PHY while
maintaining both high fidelity and transmission efficiency.
A. Framework Overview
Our framework addresses the dual challenges via two core
innovations, as illustrated in Fig. 3. First, we enable the
digital WiFi PHY to emulate analog waveform transmission
via PHY-layer inversion. Second, we enable gradient-based
training through a differentiable proxy network that simulates
the complete communication link.
B. Waveform Emulation via PHY Inversion
Our key insight is that a WiFi OFDM signal is a composite
waveform constructed from many orthogonal subcarriers. By
carefully selecting input bits to the WiFi TX PHY , we can
control each subcarrier’s amplitude and phase, orchestrating
them to collectively approximate the target analog JSCC
waveforms(t). This is achieved by two SDMs that perform
PHY-layer inversion.
As shown in Fig. 3(a), the sender-side SDM takes the target
waveforms(t)as input and computationally reverses the WiFi
TX operations (fromT 6back toT 1) to find a specific bit
sequenceb∗that, when fed to the standard WiFi TX PHY ,
produces an output waveformˆs(t)approximatings(t). The
receiver-side SDM reverses the WiFi RX PHY process (from
R1back toR 6) and employs a DNN-based recovery model to
compensate for distortion, ensuring high-fidelity input to the
JSCC decoder.
1) Overcoming Non-Invertible Channel Coding:The WiFi
convolutional encoder (T 2) poses a critical challenge: it per-
forms a many-to-one mapping that is fundamentally non-
invertible. For a desired outputy, a corresponding inputx
is not guaranteed to exist, potentially breaking the entire
inversion chain.
We model the channel coding as a linear transformation
over GF(2):
Cα×β·xβ×1=yα×1 (6)

whereβ=R·N·log2Mandα=N·log2MforNdata
subcarriers,M-QAM modulation, and coding rateR <1.
SinceR <1, the matrixCis ”tall” (α > β) and does not
have full rank, making arbitraryypotentially unsolvable.
Our solution transforms this impossibility into guaranteed
solvability by constraining emulation to a subsetN′< Nof
data subcarriers. This reduces the problem to:

whereC′contains only the rows corresponding to our chosen
subcarriers andα′=N′·log2M(see Fig. 4). This system
has a solution for anyy′ifC′has full row-rank, which is

N′≤R·N(8)
By adhering to this bound, we ensure a validxalways
exists. The unused(N−N′)subcarriers become “dummy”
carriers that the receiver-side SDM discards during reconstruc-
tion. This converts non-invertibility into a manageable trade-
off between emulation bandwidth and coding rate.
2) Distortion Compensation:Even with successful inver-
sion, the waveform suffers inevitable distortion from three
sources: (1) quantization error from mapping continuous
symbols to discrete QAM constellations, (2) cyclic prefix
insertion that overwrites portions of the target waveform,
and (3) uncontrollable pilot subcarriers with fixed values.
These distortions share a key property: they exhibit multi-scale
periodicity synchronized with both the WiFi OFDM symbol
period and the JSCC symbol period.
We exploit this structure through a DNN-based compensa-
tion network inspired by TimesNet [11]. The network trans-
forms the 1D received waveform ˜s1D∈RNs×2into two 2D
representations that expose the periodic patterns:
˜si
2D=Reshapei(Padding( ˜s1D)), i∈ {O, J}(9)
whereOdenotes OFDM periodicity andJdenotes JSCC
periodicity. A shared 2D convolutional networkf conv(·;θ comp)
processes both representations, and the outputs are aggregated:

C. ProxyNet for End-to-End Training
The WiFi PHY’s non-differentiable operations prevent di-
rect end-to-end training of the JSCC model. Our solution is
ProxyNet, a differentiable neural network trained to simulate
the complete SDM-enhanced WiFi link behavior.
1) Architecture:ProxyNet consists of three sequential mod-
ules: a sender netf send(·;θ send)that mimics the sender-side
SDM and WiFi TX PHY , a parameter-free but differentiable
channel net that injects AWGN, and a receiver netf recv(·;θ recv)
that models the WiFi RX PHY , receiver-side SDM, and
distortion compensation. Both sender and receiver nets use
U-Net architectures. The complete forward pass is:
ˆs1D=g proxy(s1D;θsend, θrecv)
=f recv(fsend(s1D;θsend) +n;θ recv)(12)
ProxyNet is trained on input-output pairs from the real
SDM-enhanced link:

2) Three-Stage Training Strategy:We employ a multi-
stage approach to jointly optimize the JSCC model, distortion
compensation network, and ProxyNet:
Stage 1: Distortion compensation initialization. We train
the compensation network by transmitting known waveforms
through the link and minimizing:
Lcomp=∥s′
1D−s 1D∥2
2 (14)
Stage 2: ProxyNet initialization. Using the pre-trained com-
pensation network, we train ProxyNet on collected link input-
output pairs:
Lproxy=∥ˆs1D−s′
1D∥2
2 (15)
Stage 3: Alternating joint optimization. We iteratively al-
ternate between two phases until convergence. First, with the
frozen ProxyNet, we train the JSCC model and fine-tune the
compensation network end-to-end by minimizing:
Ltotal=L JSCC+γL comp (16)
whereL JSCC =∥I− ˆI∥2
2is the image reconstruction loss
andγ∈[0,1]balances the objectives. Second, with frozen
JSCC and compensation networks, we generate new wave-
forms using the updated encoder, transmit them through the
real link, and fine-tune ProxyNet on these fresh samples to
maintain accurate link simulation. This alternating strategy
ensures ProxyNet continuously adapts to the evolving JSCC
model while enabling true end-to-end gradient flow for joint
optimization.

Fig. 5. MSE between source and reconstructed images versus SNR.
IV. EXPERIMENTALRESULTS
A. Experimental Setup
We evaluate our framework on semantic image transmission
over a WiFi link. Using the MNIST dataset as a proof-
of-concept benchmark, we implement the DNN components
(JSCC models and ProxyNet) in PyTorch and train on an
NVIDIA A6000 GPU. The SDM-enhanced WiFi link sim-
ulates IEEE 802.11a non-HT transmission over a 20 MHz
channel using MCS 7 (64-QAM with 5/6 coding rate), im-
plemented in MATLAB using WLAN toolboxes.
We compare against three baselines: (1)Ideal JSCCas-
sumes a perfect analog PHY that directly transmits continuous-
valued waveforms, representing the unattainable performance
ceiling free from hardware-software mismatch. (2)JSCC +
STEtrains the JSCC model end-to-end over the WiFi link by
serializing floating-point encoder outputs into bits, using the
straight-through estimator (STE) for gradient approximation
through non-differentiable PHY operations. (3)JSCC (zero-
shot)deploys an AWGN-trained JSCC model directly on the
WiFi link without adaptation, quantifying the severe degrada-
tion caused by hardware-software mismatch.
B. Performance Evaluation
Fig. 5 plots the MSE between source and reconstructed
images over SNR ranging from -5 dB to 35 dB. The results re-
veal several critical insights. First, both “Ideal JSCC” and our
method exhibit graceful degradation as SNR decreases—the
hallmark of true JSCC systems. Our MSE curve closely tracks
the ideal baseline across the full SNR range, with the small
gap attributable to unavoidable hardware-induced distortions
(quantization error, cyclic prefix mismatch, pilot interference)
inherent to the WiFi PHY . This near-ideal performance vali-
dates our framework’s effectiveness in bringing analog JSCC
benefits to practical digital implementations.
Second, the “JSCC + STE” baseline suffers from the classic
“cliff effect,” maintaining high MSE below 15 dB SNRbefore dropping sharply. This validates the channel coding
degeneracy problem described in Section II-C: the WiFi PHY’s
channel coding forces the JSCC model to degenerate into
simple source coding, recreating a separate source-channel
system whose performance is dictated entirely by the channel
coding’s operating threshold. Below this threshold, reconstruc-
tion fails catastrophically. Our method successfully avoids this
separation.
Third, the “JSCC (zero-shot)” baseline demonstrates catas-
trophic failure due to hardware-software mismatch. In the
low-to-mid SNR range (up to 10 dB), its MSE is extremely
high and counter-intuitively worsens as SNR improves. The
AWGN-trained model misinterprets the structural WiFi PHY
distortions as overwhelming noise; as actual channel noise
decreases with rising SNR, these un-learned hardware artifacts
become dominant. The sharp performance improvement when
SNR>10 dB reflects the WiFi PHY’s channel coding estab-
lishing a near-perfect digital link, not JSCC model success.
Fig. 6 provides qualitative validation through visual compar-
ison of reconstructed images across the SNR range. Both our
method and “Ideal JSCC” demonstrate smooth, progressive
quality improvement as SNR increases. At low SNRs (e.g.,
-5 dB), images remain blurry but retain coarse structural
shape; as SNR improves, digits become increasingly clear and
recognizable. This exemplifies well-functioning JSCC, where
information quality gracefully scales with channel quality. Our
visual quality closely tracks the ideal baseline at any given
SNR.
The “JSCC + STE” baseline exhibits a hard cliff ef-
fect, failing to reconstruct recognizable images below 15 dB
SNR—outputs are merely blurry blobs. At the 15 dB threshold,
quality suddenly improves to clear digits, confirming that
performance is dictated by the underlying WiFi channel coding
rather than joint optimization.
The “JSCC (zero-shot)” baseline provides the most dramatic
illustration of hardware-software mismatch. Its reconstruction
quality catastrophically collapses as SNR increases from -
5 dB to 10 dB. Instead of becoming clearer, digit structure
disintegrates into disconnected, high-contrast splotches. This
counter-intuitive collapse occurs because the AWGN-trained
model misinterprets structured WiFi PHY distortion as noise.
As actual channel noise decreases, these un-modeled artifacts
dominate and the decoder fails completely. Quality only recov-
ers for SNR>10 dB when WiFi channel coding establishes
a near-error-free link—not because the JSCC model functions
correctly.
These results demonstrate that our framework successfully
emulates classic analog JSCC systems’ graceful degradation
while operating on entirely digital hardware. We make a
strategic trade-off: accepting a minor performance ceiling at
high SNR (due to residual emulation error) in exchange for
unparalleled robustness in noisy, variable environments. This
inherent resilience makes our method fundamentally more
reliable and practical for real-world communication systems
than conventional digital approaches optimized for narrow,
ideal conditions.

ACKNOWLEDGMENT
This work is supported by the National Science and Tech-
nology Major Project - Mobile Information Networks under
Grant No. 2024ZD1300700, and by National Natural Science
Foundation of China with grant 62301471.
V. CONCLUSION
This paper introduced D2AJSCC, a novel framework that
bridges the critical gap between analog JSCC’s demonstrated
advantages and practical deployment on ubiquitous digital
hardware. By exploiting OFDM’s parallel subcarrier structure
as a waveform synthesizer, our computational PHY inversion
approach enables high-fidelity emulation of continuous-valued
analog waveforms using standard digital PHYs. Our proxy-
based training strategy, centered on ProxyNet as a differ-
entiable neural surrogate, enables true end-to-end gradient
flow while preventing JSCC degeneration caused by non-
differentiable PHY operations. Simulation results demonstrate
that our system achieves near-ideal analog JSCC performance
with graceful degradation across SNR conditions, successfully
avoiding the cliff effects that plague conventional digital
approaches. This work establishes a practical pathway for de-
ploying the superior robustness and efficiency of analog JSCC
on modern communication hardware, fostering a sustainable
ecosystem where advanced semantic capabilities coexist with
legacy infrastructure.
REFERENCES
[1] S. Guo, Y . Wang, N. Zhang, Z. Su, T. H. Luan, Z. Tian, and X. Shen,
“A survey on semantic communication networks: Architecture, security,
and privacy,”IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials, pp. 1–1, 2024.
[2] D. G ¨und¨uz, M. A. Wigger, T.-Y . Tung, P. Zhang, and Y . Xiao, “Joint
source–channel coding: Fundamentals and recent progress in practical
designs,”Proceedings of the IEEE, pp. 1–32, 2024.
[3] Y . Fu, W. Cheng, W. Zhang, and J. Wang, “Digital-analog transmission
framework for task-oriented semantic communications,”IEEE Network,
vol. 38, no. 6, pp. 81–88, 2024.[4] E. Bourtsoulatze, D. B. Kurka, and D. G ¨und¨uz, “Deep joint source-
channel coding for wireless image transmission,”IEEE Transactions on
Cognitive Communications and Networking, vol. 5, no. 3, pp. 567–579,
2019.
[5] P. Jiang, C.-K. Wen, S. Jin, and G. Y . Li, “Deep source-channel coding
for sentence semantic transmission with harq,”IEEE transactions on
communications, vol. 70, no. 8, pp. 5225–5240, 2022.
[6] W. Zhang, S. Wu, S. Meng, J. He, and Q. Zhang, “Engineering a
lightweight deep joint source-channel-coding-based semantic communi-
cation system,”IEEE Internet of Things Journal, vol. 12, no. 1, pp. 458–
471, 2025.
[7] T.-Y . Tung, D. B. Kurka, M. Jankowski, and D. G ¨und¨uz, “Deepjscc-
q: Constellation constrained deep joint source-channel coding,”IEEE
Journal on Selected Areas in Information Theory, vol. 3, no. 4, pp. 720–
731, 2022.
[8] Y . Bo, Y . Duan, S. Shao, and M. Tao, “Joint coding-modulation for

[9] J. Huang, K. Yuan, C. Huang, and K. Huang, “D²-jscc: Digital deep joint

[10] Q. Fu, H. Xie, Z. Qin, G. Slabaugh, and X. Tao, “Vector quantized se-

vol. 12, no. 6, pp. 982–986, 2023.
[11] H. Wu, T. Hu, Y . Liu, H. Zhou, J. Wang, and M. Long, “Timesnet:
Temporal 2d-variation modeling for general time series analysis,” inThe

structure.title: Unlocking High-Fidelity Analog Joint Source-Channel Coding on Standard Digital Transceivers
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of analog joint source-channel coding (JSCC) and its potential for semantic communications. The main points covered include the introduction of a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital physical layers (PHYs). The document highlights the challenges of deploying analog JSCC on digital hardware, including signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers. It also presents a solution using orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing and a differentiable neural surrogate called ProxyNet to enable end-to-end training. The document demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework through simulation results for image transmission over WiFi PHY, showing near-ideal analog JSCC performance with graceful degradation across signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

nodes.title: Abstract
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of analog joint source-channel coding (JSCC) and its potential for semantic communications. The main points covered include the introduction of a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital physical layers (PHYs). The document highlights the challenges of deploying analog JSCC on digital hardware, including signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers. It also presents a solution using orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing and a differentiable neural surrogate called ProxyNet to enable end-to-end training. The document demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework through simulation results for image transmission over WiFi PHY, showing near-ideal analog JSCC performance with graceful degradation across SNR conditions. Overall, the document aims to bridge the gap between analog JSCC's theoretical promise and practical deployment on ubiquitous digital hardware, promoting sustainable network evolution.

nodes.title: Contributions
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of analog joint source-channel coding (JSCC) and its potential for semantic communications. The main points covered include the introduction of a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital physical layers (PHYs). The framework addresses two fundamental challenges: signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers. The authors propose a waveform emulation approach and a proxy-based training strategy to overcome these challenges. The document also presents a system model and problem formulation, and validates the framework through simulations of image transmission over a standard WiFi PHY. The key contributions include introducing a waveform emulation approach, developing a proxy-based training strategy, and demonstrating the practical viability of the framework. The overall goal is to bridge the gap between analog JSCC's theoretical promise and practical deployment on digital hardware, promoting sustainable network evolution and enabling next-generation semantic transmission on legacy infrastructure.

structure.title: System Model and Problem Formulation
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) and its limitations in digital Physical Layer (PHY) systems. The main points covered include the challenges of implementing analog JSCC in digital hardware, such as non-differentiable operations and signal format incompatibility. The document introduces a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital PHYs. The key contributions of the framework include a waveform emulation approach, a proxy-based training strategy, and experimental validation via simulations of image transmission over a standard WiFi PHY. The document also provides background on analog JSCC, the WiFi PHY, and the core deployment challenges addressed by the framework. Overall, the partial document presents a solution to bridge the gap between analog JSCC's conceptual power and digital hardware's practical constraints.

nodes.title: Problem Formulation
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses the challenges of deploying analog Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) over standard digital Physical Layer (PHY) hardware, such as WiFi. The main points covered include: 

1. The limitations of existing solutions, including quantization-based approaches and digital JSCC schemes, in addressing the incompatibility between analog JSCC and digital PHYs.
2. The introduction of a novel framework, D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital PHYs by addressing two fundamental challenges: signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers.
3. The description of the D2AJSCC framework, which consists of two core innovations: waveform emulation via PHY-layer inversion using software-defined modules (SDMs), and a differentiable proxy network (ProxyNet) that simulates the complete communication link, enabling end-to-end training through the non-differentiable physical link.
4. The formulation of the problem, including the system model and the two challenges that the framework addresses, and the objective of designing a framework that enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment over standard WiFi PHY while preserving graceful degradation and transmission efficiency.

structure.title: Proposed Framework
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the deployment of analog Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) over a standard WiFi Physical Layer (PHY). The main points covered include the WiFi transmitter and receiver processing chains, the challenges of deploying analog JSCC over a standard WiFi PHY, such as signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers, and a proposed framework to address these challenges. The framework consists of two core innovations: waveform emulation via PHY-layer inversion using software-defined modules (SDMs) and a differentiable proxy network that simulates the complete communication link. The document also touches on the technical details of the framework, including overcoming non-invertible channel coding and using deep neural networks (DNNs) for distortion recovery.

nodes.title: Architecture
nodes.summary: This partial document discusses the concept of analog joint source-channel coding (JSCC) and its potential for semantic communications. The main points covered include the introduction of a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which enables high-fidelity analog JSCC deployment on standard digital physical layers (PHYs). The framework addresses two fundamental challenges: signal format incompatibility and non-differentiable training barriers. The solution involves waveform emulation via PHY inversion using software-defined modules (SDMs) and a differentiable proxy network called ProxyNet, which enables end-to-end training through the non-differentiable physical link. The document also presents a system model and problem formulation, followed by a detailed explanation of the proposed framework, including waveform emulation, distortion compensation, and ProxyNet architecture. The goal of the framework is to achieve high-fidelity analog JSCC performance with graceful degradation across signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions, while promoting sustainable network evolution and bridging the gap between analog JSCC's theoretical promise and practical deployment on ubiquitous digital hardware.

nodes.title: Three-Stage Training Strategy
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a method for solving non-invertibility in a system by constraining emulation to a subset of subcarriers, creating a square, full-rank system. It also introduces a distortion compensation network to mitigate inevitable distortion from sources such as quantization error and cyclic prefix insertion. The document then describes ProxyNet, a differentiable neural network trained to simulate the complete SDM-enhanced WiFi link behavior, allowing for end-to-end training of the JSCC model. The method is evaluated on semantic image transmission over a WiFi link, demonstrating near-ideal performance and avoiding the "cliff effect" and hardware-software mismatch issues that plague other approaches. The results show that the proposed framework successfully emulates classic analog JSCC systems' graceful degradation while operating on entirely digital hardware, making it more reliable and practical for real-world communication systems.

structure.title: Experimental Results
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the experimental results of a framework for semantic image transmission over a WiFi link. The framework is evaluated using the MNIST dataset and compared to three baselines: Ideal JSCC, JSCC + STE, and JSCC (zero-shot). The results show that the proposed framework exhibits graceful degradation as SNR decreases, closely tracking the ideal baseline, while the other baselines suffer from the "cliff effect" or catastrophic failure due to hardware-software mismatch. The framework's effectiveness is validated through quantitative and qualitative evaluations, demonstrating its ability to bring analog JSCC benefits to practical digital implementations and making it more reliable and practical for real-world communication systems.

nodes.title: Experimental Setup
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the experimental results of a framework for semantic image transmission over a WiFi link using a deep neural network (DNN) components and joint source-channel coding (JSCC) models. The main points covered include the experimental setup, performance evaluation, and comparison with three baselines: Ideal JSCC, JSCC + STE, and JSCC (zero-shot). The results show that the proposed framework exhibits graceful degradation as SNR decreases, closely tracking the ideal baseline, and avoids the "cliff effect" and catastrophic failure due to hardware-software mismatch. The document also provides qualitative validation through visual comparison of reconstructed images, demonstrating the framework's effectiveness in bringing analog JSCC benefits to practical digital implementations and its inherent resilience in noisy, variable environments.

nodes.title: Performance Evaluation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the experimental results of a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which bridges the gap between analog Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) and practical deployment on digital hardware. The framework is evaluated on semantic image transmission over a WiFi link using the MNIST dataset. The results show that the proposed method achieves near-ideal analog JSCC performance with graceful degradation across Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) conditions, avoiding the cliff effects that plague conventional digital approaches. The method is compared to three baselines: Ideal JSCC, JSCC + STE, and JSCC (zero-shot), and it outperforms them in terms of Mean Squared Error (MSE) and visual quality of reconstructed images. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in emulating classic analog JSCC systems while operating on digital hardware, making it a reliable and practical solution for real-world communication systems.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel framework called D2AJSCC, which bridges the gap between analog Joint Source-Channel Coding (JSCC) and practical deployment on digital hardware. The main points covered include the introduction of D2AJSCC, its ability to emulate continuous-valued analog waveforms using standard digital PHYs, and its proxy-based training strategy. The document also presents simulation results demonstrating the system's near-ideal analog JSCC performance and graceful degradation across Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) conditions. Additionally, the document acknowledges the support of the National Science and Technology Major Project and the National Natural Science Foundation of China, and provides a list of references related to semantic communication networks, joint source-channel coding, and deep learning-based approaches.

The conclusion summarizes the main contributions of the paper, including the introduction of D2AJSCC, its ability to achieve near-ideal analog JSCC performance, and its potential to deploy analog JSCC on modern communication hardware.
